body_txt: Oftentimes, the Hawaiian-Shirted Tourist will appear as a fixture in pop culture to showcase that they're an awkward, unsophisticated, or otherwise generic tourist and are on vacation (regardless of if it's relevant to actually wear a Hawaiian shirt, i.e., in the middle of Paris). Otherwise, a character will sport a Hawaiian shirt (also known as an Aloha shirt) to display that they're outside of the norm. This is usually as the Foil to the more straight-laced—and often more conservatively dressed—characters, which can include the Cloud Cuckoolander , the Plucky Comic Relief , or even one of the main characters (especially if they're a part of a duo or an ensemble cast). Red appears to be the most popular color and although women do sport the shirts, for some reason in media, it's almost always seen on men. Also expect that whenever they're not wearing the shirt, that their personality and/or the situation is markedly more serious. Super-Trope of Fun T-Shirt and Consistent Clothing Style . Compare and contrast with Wardrobe Flaw of Characterization . See also Tacky Tuxedo , Quirky Girl, Quirky Tux , Goofy Print Underwear , Impossibly Tacky Clothes , Clothes Make the Legend , Iconic Item and Impossibly Cool Clothes . Please don't confuse this with a bowling shirt; while this can be formal at times, bowler's clothing is almost always informal. Also, explain how the shirt wearer would qualify as this trope; hard as it is to believe, just because someone wears a Hawaiian shirt doesn't automatically make them quirky.
